title: Crimes Against Humanity Are Never Legally Binding
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that Roe v. Wade was a crime against humanity that state governments had a moral duty to nullify, drawing a direct parallel between pro-life politicians' deference to Roe and the Nazi 'I was just following orders' defense rejected at Nuremberg. The article anchors nullificationism in higher law, the Kentucky Resolutions, and a Christian moral framework.
audiences: Pro-life politicians and lobbyists who defer to Roe
Secular readers unfamiliar with higher-law arguments

questions.question: What is the 'Nuremberg Defense' and why does Silberman invoke it against pro-life politicians?
questions.answer: Silberman uses the Nazi defense of 'an order is an order' as a moral analogue for pro-life officials who refuse to abolish abortion because they say they must obey the Supreme Court. The point is that obedience to an unjust order is never a legitimate moral cover.
questions.quote: When Nazi war criminals went before an international court in Nuremberg, the primary legal defenses were versions of “befehl ist befehl” – “an order is an order” or “I was just following orders.” This plea is now known as the “Nuremberg Defense.”

questions.question: On what grounds does Silberman claim that any government order to commit murder is automatically void?
questions.answer: He appeals to a higher law above human law—what the Declaration of Independence calls 'the law of nature and nature's God'—which renders any command to murder the innocent null at its inception.
questions.quote: Many nazis were executed and rightly so because there is a law higher than man’s law. The Declaration of Independence refers to it as the law “of nature and nature’s God.” Any government order to murder the innocent is null by its nature because it is in violation of the highest law.

questions.question: How does psychological research on coercion reinforce the article's moral argument?
questions.audience: Secular readers unfamiliar with higher-law arguments
questions.answer: Silberman cites Milgram and a UCL/Brussels study showing that people who act under orders psychologically detach from responsibility, which he says explains how pro-life politicians can enable abortion while feeling morally clean.
questions.quote: actions taken under coercion decreases one’s “sense of agency.” The report further explained that “people who obey orders may subjectively experience their actions as closer to passive movements than fully voluntary actions.”

questions.question: What distinction do pro-life lawyers draw between Roe and the Nazi orders, and how does Silberman answer it?
questions.audience: Pro-life politicians and lobbyists who defer to Roe
questions.answer: Pro-lifers argue Roe merely permits abortion rather than commanding it, like China's one-child policy. Silberman counters that permitting mass murder is itself a crime against humanity that legislators are obligated to nullify.
questions.quote: “The logic of [comparing submission to *Roe* to submission to Nazi orders] is faulty, since Roe does not ‘order’ abortions to be carried out (like China’s’ ‘one-child’ policy) but provides for their legal availability to women as a matter of right.”

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the moral position of pro-life leaders in states they control?
questions.answer: He says they are not bystanders but active enablers of genocide, because they hold the power to abolish abortion in their states and choose not to use it.
questions.quote: Pro-life legislators and executives in states where pro-lifers control the state government are allowing crimes against humanity to take place in their states. **This genocide is enabled by the pro-life leaders.**

questions.question: What historical American authority does Silberman cite to ground a state's right to nullify federal tyranny?
questions.answer: He invokes Thomas Jefferson's Kentucky Resolutions, which held that nullification is the rightful remedy whenever the federal government acts tyrannically.
questions.quote: In the Kentucky Resolutions, Thomas Jefferson wrote that “every state has a natural right to nullify,” and that when the federal government behaves tyrannically, “nullification… is the rightful remedy.”

questions.question: Why does Silberman frame nullification as a human right and sometimes a duty rather than a mere political tactic?
questions.answer: Because the only alternative is to grant governing authorities unlimited power to inflict evil on neighbors with no recourse, which he says aligns the citizen with the Nazi defense.
questions.quote: This is why it is a human right, and sometimes a human obligation, to nullify when a governing authority commands that which is evil, and especially if that command rises to the level of being a crime against humanity.

questions.question: How does the article connect its moral argument to a Christian theological foundation?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that the very categories of good and evil presuppose the Christian God revealed in Romans 1, and that atheism cannot account for the moral indignation we all feel toward atrocities like abortion.
questions.quote: The simple act of calling something “good” or “evil” is to borrow from the Christian worldview. Atheism cannot justify these concepts.

questions.question: What scale of harm does the article attribute to Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Secular readers unfamiliar with higher-law arguments
questions.answer: Silberman calls Roe one of history's greatest mass murders, citing more than 63 million dead, and argues this scale obligates state governments to stop treating it as binding law.
questions.quote: *Roe v. Wade* is a crime against humanity that ushered in one of the greatest mass murders the world has ever seen with 63+ million dead and counting.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: The flagship Abolitionists Rising statement on nullification, which provides the theoretical backbone the article applies to Roe.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: A companion Silberman essay defending nullification's historical pedigree against the same constitutional objections raised by pro-life lawyers here.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: A concrete case study of the 'befehl ist befehl' posture among pro-life legislators that this article condemns.

related.title: Arkansas SB6: "Pleading" When We Should Be Defying
related.reason: Published the same week and same author, contrasting pleading-with-courts strategies against the defiance/nullification posture this article demands.

related.title: First Ever Bill to Ignore Roe and Abolish Abortion in Colorado: HB22-1079
related.reason: A legislative example of the 'Ignore Roe' strategy this article calls for, treating Roe as the legal nullity Silberman describes.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Extends the critique of pro-life leaders who rationalize submission to Roe, the same posture this article likens to the Nuremberg Defense.
